mnemonics.title: SARSA STATES SEQUENTIALLY
mnemonics.content: SARSA = State-Action-Reward-State-Action - This reminds you that SARSA is an ON-POLICY algorithm that learns the value of the policy being followed, using the actual next action taken.

mnemonics.title: Q-LEARNING QUICKLY LEARNS QUALITY
mnemonics.content: Q in Q-Learning stands for Quality - representing the quality (expected return) of taking an action in a given state. It's OFF-POLICY and learns the optimal value function.

mnemonics.title: Dqn DEEP QUALITY NETWORK
mnemonics.content: DQN = Deep Q-Network - Uses DEEP neural networks to approximate Q-values, with EXPERIENCE REPLAY and TARGET NETWORKS for stability.

acronyms.expansion: Reinforcement Learning - Learning through interaction with environment to maximize cumulative reward
acronyms.usage: Used when agents must learn sequential decision-making through trial and error

acronyms.expansion: Markov Decision Process - Mathematical framework with states, actions, transitions, and rewards
acronyms.usage: Formal model for sequential decision problems in RL

acronyms.expansion: Deep Q-Network - Q-Learning with deep neural networks for function approximation
acronyms.usage: Applying deep learning to handle high-dimensional state spaces like images

acronyms.expansion: Temporal Difference - Learning method combining Monte Carlo and Dynamic Programming
acronyms.usage: Updates value estimates based on difference between predictions and actual rewards
Learns value of the policy being followed, useful when exploration matters

acronyms.expansion: Greedy in the Front - Epsilon-greedy exploration strategy
acronyms.usage: Reminds that with probability epsilon we act greedily in the front (random exploration)

acronyms.expansion: GENERALLY ACTUALLY MAKES FUTURE REWARDS LESS IMPORTANT - Discount factor
acronyms.usage: Gamma close to 0 means immediate rewards matter more, gamma near 1 means future matters equally

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a heat map of a grid world where each cell's color represents expected cumulative reward from that state - darker colors mean better states. The agent navigates toward darker cells.
Think of a spreadsheet where rows are states and columns are actions. Each cell contains the expected future reward for that state-action pair. The agent picks the highest value.
Picture a restaurant you know (exploitation - guaranteed good meal) vs trying a new restaurant (exploration - might be better or worse). The epsilon parameter controls how often you take the risk.
Imagine rewards as money. Gamma = 0.9 means getting $100 tomorrow is like getting $90 today. Future rewards lose value over time, just like money loses purchasing power.
Think of training a dog with treats - reward signals (treats) reinforce good behavior. The timing and amount of reward shape the learning.

analogies.analogy: It's like learning to play a video game without a manual. You try moves, see what happens (rewards/penalties), gradually learn which button combinations lead to winning, even though consequences of moves might be far in the future.

Like trying to figure out which player in a soccer team deserves credit for a goal scored in the 90th minute - was it the pass in the 30th minute? The shot? The strategy? The agent must trace back which actions led to the final reward.
Think of value function like a GPS that doesn't just show next turn, but estimates total time to destination from every intersection - helping make globally optimal decisions.
Policy is like an employee's handbook - it tells you exactly what to do in each situation. The learning process writes and improves this handbook based on outcomes.

It's exactly like deciding whether to stick with your favorite restaurant (exploitation) or try that new place everyone talks about (exploration). You balance guaranteed satisfaction with the chance of discovery.
